coredata.dc:description: Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots (SmCDs), as a new type of nanomaterial, play vital roles in plant growth, antioxidation and abiotic stress alleviation. Flowering Chinese cabbage is prone to wilt and yellowing after harvest. However, the roles of SmCDs in delaying senescence of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage to maintain quality remain unclear. Herein, we found that SmCDs had effective impacts on anti-aging, enhancing maximal fluorescence ratio, procrastinating chlorophyll degradation, sustaining reactive oxygen species (ROS) metabolism homeostasis, and improving the contents of vitamin C, sucrose, soluble sugar, and flavonoid. Moreover, SmCDs suppressed the expressions of chlorophyll degradation genes (BrNYE1, BrNYC1, BrNOL, BrPPH, and BrPAO), senescence marker gene (BrSAG12), and respiratory burst oxidase homologs genes (BrRBohA, BrRBohB, BrRBohD, and BrRBohF), while SmCDs promoted the flavonoid biosynthesis genes (BrFLS3, BrFLS1, BrC4H, and BrCHI) expression. Interestingly, sucrose synthase, neutral invertase, and acid invertase might be the pivotal enzymes to restrict sugar accumulation under SmCDs treatment.

Vertical bars indicate the standard error of the mean (n = 3). The asterisks above the columns indicate a significant difference at P < 0.05.

Effects of SmCDs on the expressions of chlorophyll degradation-related genes BrPAO (A), BrPPH (B), BrNYC1 (C), BrNOL (D), BrNYE1 (E), BrRCCR (G), and senescence marker gene BrSAG12 (F) in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage at 4 ℃ during storage. CK, Control. SmCDs, 1 mg/mL Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots treatment. Vertical bars indicate the standard error of the mean (n = 3). The asterisks above the columns indicate a significant difference at P < 0.05.

Effects of SmCDs on the reactive oxygen metabolism in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage at 4 ℃ during storage. Nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) and diaminobenzidine (DAB) staining (A), Cell membrane permeability (B), Superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity (C), Peroxidase (POD) activity (D), Catalase (CAT) activity (E), Aseorbateperoxidase (APX) activity (F), Lipoxygenase (LOX) activity (G), Malonaldehyde (MDA) content (H), Proline (Pro) content (I). CK, Control. SmCDs, 1 mg/mL Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots treatment. The vertical bars indicate the standard error of the mean (n = 3). The asterisks above the columns indicate a significant difference at P < 0.05.

CK, Control. SmCDs, 1 mg/mL Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots treatment. Vertical bars indicate the standard error of the mean (n = 3). The asterisks above the columns indicate a significant difference at P < 0.05.

Effects of SmCDs on the quality of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage at 4 ℃ during storage. (A) Weight loss rate. (B) Vitamin C content. (C) Soluble sugar content. (D) Sucrose content. (E) Flavonoid content. CK, Control. SmCDs, 1 mg/mL Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots treatment. Vertical bars indicate the standard error of the mean (n = 3). The asterisks above columns indicate a significant difference at P < 0.05.

Effects of SmCDs on the activities of sugar metabolism-related enzymes of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage at 4 ℃ during storage. (A) Neutral invertase (NI) activity. (B) Acid invertase (AI) activity. (C) Sucrose synthase (SS) activity. (D) Sucrose phosphate synthase (SPS) activity. CK, Control. SmCDs, 1 mg/mL Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots treatment. Vertical bars indicate the standard error of the mean (n = 3). The asterisks above the columns indicate a significant difference at P < 0.05.

Effects of SmCDs on the expressions of flavonoid biosynthesis-related genes BrFLS3 (A), BrFLS1 (B), BrC4H (C), BrCHI (D), BrDFR (E), BrCHS (F), and BrTT7 (G) in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage at 4 ℃ during storage. CK, Control. SmCDs, 1 mg/mL Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots treatment. Vertical bars indicate the standard error of the mean (n = 3). The asterisks above columns indicate a significant difference at P < 0.05.

head.ce:title: The roles of Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots involving in maintaining quality by delaying senescence of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots (SmCDs), as a new type of nanomaterial, play vital roles in plant growth, antioxidation and abiotic stress alleviation. Flowering Chinese cabbage is prone to wilt and yellowing after harvest. However, the roles of SmCDs in delaying senescence of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage to maintain quality remain unclear. Herein, we found that SmCDs had effective impacts on anti-aging, enhancing maximal fluorescence ratio, procrastinating chlorophyll degradation, sustaining reactive oxygen species (ROS) metabolism homeostasis, and improving the contents of vitamin C, sucrose, soluble sugar, and flavonoid. Moreover, SmCDs suppressed the expressions of chlorophyll degradation genes (BrNYE1, BrNYC1, BrNOL, BrPPH, and BrPAO), senescence marker gene (BrSAG12), and respiratory burst oxidase homologs genes (BrRBohA, BrRBohB, BrRBohD, and BrRBohF), while SmCDs promoted the flavonoid biosynthesis genes (BrFLS3, BrFLS1, BrC4H, and BrCHI) expression. Interestingly, sucrose synthase, neutral invertase, and acid invertase might be the pivotal enzymes to restrict sugar accumulation under SmCDs treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Vegetables are the main vitamin and nutrient providers which are indispensable necessities in people's daily life. Nevertheless, their quick withering, yellowing and deterioration after harvest result in the loss of nutrition and flavor as well as shortening shelf life, which might directly impact the commodity performance and cause economic loss (Hoppu et al., 2021). As a result, prolonging shelf life and sustaining quality attribute of harvested vegetables become focal points in the vegetable industry. Physical (Reyes Jara et al., 2022), chemical (Miao et al., 2020; Yue et al., 2022) and biological (Yu et al., 2021) preservation techniques have currently been used to prolong shelf life and preserve the quality of harvested vegetables. Among the postharvest handlings, chemical preservation is the simplest way to preserve vegetables quality with low-cost and high impacts (Yue et al., 2022). Previous studies have demonstrated that chemical handlings successfully preserved harvested vegetable quality by delaying aging through retarding ethylene production and chlorophyll degradation, activating antioxidant systems, and inhibiting pathogenic bacteria, resulting in maintaining the quality of postharvest vegetables (Tan et al., 2019; Yue et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020). Although the successes in prolonging shelf life and sustaining the quality of harvested vegetables using chemical preservation techniques have been perceived, most chemical used in these techniques can show negative effects on the physical, nutritional, or bioactive characteristics of the products, they correspondingly cause harm to human and to the environment (Pinela and Ferreira, 2017).

Salvia miltiorrhiza is one of the longest and most widely used herbal medicines presenting antioxidant activities in Asia (Tang et al., 2019). Nonetheless, its applications in plants are relatively restricted due to the low solubility in water and the large particle size of its extracts (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). At the current time, the intention of nanoparticles has been raising in developing technologies relating to biotic and abiotic stress due to their distinctive abilities such as easy entry into plants, environment friendliness, unique optical, and chemical performance (Kou et al., 2021). Carbon dots (CDs), one of the burgeoning nanoparticles, are different from other nanomaterials owing to their small size, excellent biocompatibility, photoluminescence performance, and fewer side effects, wherein these properties lead CDs to be studied in antioxidation and resistance to stress (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). Previous reports have shown that CDs derived from edible pure plant materials and extracted by non-toxic synthetic methods can completely be considered safe products (Zhang et al., 2021). As inspired by the above analysis, we used Salvia miltiorrhiza as the raw material to synthesize Salvia miltiorrhiza-derived carbon dots (SmCDS) by hydrothermal method. Recent studies have shown that SmCDs had powerful antioxidant activities than that of the pure Salvia miltiorrhiza extracts, and cytotoxicity assessment demonstrated that SmCDs are safe and display excellent biocompatibility, which can be applied in plants (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). Moreover, we correspondingly found that SmCDS could alleviate the stress of lettuce to the high-temperature and salt by enhancing the growth and promoting the antioxidant enzyme activity, and acting as a scavenger of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for decreasing oxidative injury of plants (Li et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). To date, the previous researches on the applications of SmCDs have focused mainly on the effects on pre-harvest crops, however, little research has been done on the postharvest biology of plants, and the underlying mechanisms of SmCDs maintaining the quality through delaying postharvest senescence remain largely unknown.

Flowering Chinese cabbage (Brassica campestris L. ssp. chinensis var. utilis Tsen et Lee) is one of the Chinese specialty vegetables, which is commonly cultivated in south China. In recent years, it has developed rapidly in Northwest and North China as well as has been widely transported to all regions of China. Nevertheless, flowering Chinese cabbage is quickly withering, yellowing and deterioration (remarkable disgrace in visual and nutrient qualities) after postharvest processing (a storage life of 2‐3 days in the air under ambient temperature) due to its higher content of water (Tan et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022; Yue et al., 2022). Therefore, postharvest handlings or technologies for preserving harvested flowering Chinese cabbage are urgently required. It has been reported that pre-harvest H2O2 treatment alleviated water loss and activated the antioxidant defense system, protected the chloroplast membrane from oxidative damage, and ultimately delayed senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage leaves during storage (Wang et al., 2022). Melatonin could effectively postpone the aging of flowering Chinese cabbage leaves by hindering abscisic acid biosynthesis, resisting chlorophyll decomposition, and maintaining ROS homeostasis (Tan et al., 2019, 2021). Furthermore, the exogenous application of 6-benzylaminopurine has significantly been found to retard the senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage leaves, with less chlorophyll decomposition and lower ROS accumulation and malondialdehyde content compared with the control group (distilled water) (Wang et al., 2022). Our recent studies also showed that 1-methylcyclopropene (1-MCP), benzoic acid, and chlorine dioxide could delay aging and preserve the postharvest quality of flowering Chinese cabbage (Yue et al., 2022). So far, the CDs extending shelf life and maintaining quality have not been explored in harvested vegetables or postharvest technologies. Thus, this study aims to investigate the impacts of SmCDs on the senescence and quality of harvested flowering Chinese cabbage. The results of our work revealed that SmCDs could effectively retard the chlorophyll degradation and the transcript of senescence marker gene, improve the activity of antioxidant enzymes, inhibit the excessive production of ROS, reduce the membrane lipid peroxidation, protect cell membrane from injury, resulting in delaying the senescence and promoting the contents of vitamin C, sucrose, soluble sugar, and flavonoid. Our findings initially confirmed that SmCDs were involved in maintaining the postharvest quality by delaying the aging of flowering Chinese cabbage, which greatly assisted in the development of new postharvest handling method to prolong the shelf life of horticultural crops.

SmCDs were synthesized following the previous reported method (Wang et al., 2021). Briefly, the fresh samples of Salvia miltiorrhiza were dried to constant weight in an electric blast drying box (Yiheng, Shanghai, China) at 50 °C, then crushed into powder. 15 g of Salvia miltiorrhiza powder and 300 mL of double-distilled water were added to a beaker, followed by treatment with ultrasonication (Bio-analysis Ultrasonic Instrument Corp., Shanghai, China) for 30 min. Then, the suspension was transferred to a 500 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (Trina Testing Technology Corp., Shandong, China) and heated at 180 °C for 6 h, and the resulting mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 20 min (25 °C). After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected and purified with a 0.22 μm filter membrane (Zinteng, Tianjin, China). The resulting solution was used to remove small molecules by a cellulose dialysis bag (1000 Da; Viskase, Illinois, USA), freeze-dried, and then stored at 4 °C until needed.

The fresh samples of flowering Chinese cabbage were harvested from vegetable farm of Huachuang Agriculture Corp. (Zhongshan, Guangdong, China) on 13 December 2021. In this experiment, two treatment groups were set up: control (double-distilled water, CK) and 1 mg/mL SmCDs treatment. It was ascertained in a previous pilot test that 1 mg/mL SmCDs were more effective to postpone aging of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage than treating with 0.5, 1.5, 2, and 5 mg/mL (Fig. S1). Thus, 1 mg/mL SmCDs were chose in the following test. Each treatment group consisted of three repetitions and 10 plants per repetition. The control group was sprayed with double-distilled water. The SmCDs treatment was sprayed with 1 mg/mL concentration. The application dosage was 0.01 L per repetition. After spraying, the samples were placed into a polyethylene bag (40 cm × 30 cm) and sealed. Then, the samples were stored at 4 °C for 21 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of chlorophyll content and maximal fluorescence (Fv/Fm) ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The total chlorophyll content was assayed following the method of Arkus et al. (2005). Briefly, randomly weighed 0.5 g of leaves and put them into a 50 mL centrifuge tube, and added 25 mL premix of acetone and ethanol (v:v = 2:1), then soaked in the dark for 24 h until the leaves were completely discolored, finally took out the supernatant and measured the absorbance values at 645 nm and 663 nm. The ratio of Fv/Fm was assayed non-invasively by a chlorophyll fluorometer (Imaging-PAM-M series, Heinz Walz, Germany) following the manufacturer’s instructions (Behr et al., 2010).

The weight loss was evaluated following the method of Yu et al. (2022) and expressed as percentages. It was calculated following the formula: Weight loss (%) = (m0 - m1)/ m0 × 100 %. The m0 was the weight before storage, and the m1 was the weight after storage.

The contents of vitamin C, soluble sugar, and sucrose were determined according to our previous methods (Tan et al., 2022). The content of vitamin C was assayed by molybdenum blue colorimetry. Briefly, 1 g fresh samples were ground into homologues with 25 mL oxalic acid, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 r/min for 10 min (4 °C), then the supernatants were collected to assay the vitamin C content using an ultraviolet (UV)-2410PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corp., Kyoto, Japan) at 760 nm. The content of soluble sugar was assayed by anthrone colorimetry. Briefly, 1 g fresh Samples were heated in a boiling water bath with 10 mL distilled water twice for 30 min each time. The resulting extracts were mixed with potassium ferrocyanide (1 mL) and zinc acetate (1 mL). The absorbance was assayed by an UV-2410PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corp., Kyoto, Japan) at 630 nm after standing for 15 min. The content of sucrose was assayed by a Waters high-performance liquid chromatograph (HPLC) (Waters, Massachusetts, USA). Briefly, 0.5 g fresh samples were ground and swirled with 90 % ethanol (4 mL) for 30 s, placed in a water bath for 20 min at 80 °C, then centrifuged at 12,000 r/min for 10 min (4 °C). After centrifugation, the supernatants were collected and filtered through a 0.45 μm filter membrane, then transferred to a test vial. The chromatographic separation was performed on the Waters HPLC system with an Agilent NH2 column (4.6 mm × 250 mm), and the mobile phase comprised acetonitrile and water (volume ratio: 75:25). The flow rate was 1 mL min−1. The injection volume was 10 μL and the detection wavelength was 190 nm.

The flavonoid content was conducted following the previous study of Pérez-Ambrocio et al. (2018) with slight modifications. Briefly, 0.5 g fresh samples were ground into homogenate with 8 mL anhydrous ethanol. The homogenates were stood for 30 min, then centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 15 min (4 ℃). The supernatants were collected and mixed with 1.5 % NaNO2 (0.5 mL), 3 % AlCl3 (1.0 mL) and 1.0 mol/L NaOH (1.0 mL). After incubation for 5 min in the dark, the absorbance was assayed at 510 nm using an UV-2410PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corp., Kyoto, Japan).

To determine the reactive oxygen metabolism for each treatment, 5 plants from each repetition were randomly chosen. Three repetitions were carried out for each determination. The indicators of reactive oxygen metabolism were assayed following the previous reported method (Mao et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). In brief, 0.5 g leaf fresh samples were placed into a pre-cooled (4 °C) mortar, mixed with 0.3 g polyvinylpyrrolidone and 0.05 mol/L pH 7.8 phosphate buffered solution (5 mL) and ground, then centrifugated at 10,000 r/min for 20 min (4 ℃). The supernatants were collected for the assay of catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), and aseorbateperoxidase (APX), lipoxygenase (LOX), malonaldehyde (MDA) and proline (Pro). All spectrophotometric assays were conducted by a UV-2410PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corp., Kyoto, Japan). Nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) and diaminobenzidine (DAB) staining were performed following the method of Liu et al. (2017). The cell membrane permeability was determined following the method of Kang et al. (2022).

The sugar metabolism-related enzymes were evaluated following the previous reported method (Song et al., 2015). In brief, 0.2 g leaf fresh samples were placed into a pre-cooled (4 °C) mortar, mixed with 1 mL pre-chilled extraction buffers (0.1 mol/L pH 7.5 phosphate buffer, 5 mmol/L MgCl2, 1 mmol/L EDTA, 0.1 % TritonX-100, and 0.1 % β-mercaptoethanol) and ground into homogenate under ice bath, then centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 15 min (4 ℃), aspirated the supernatant in a 5 mL centrifuge tube, repeated the extraction once with 1 mL of extraction solution, combined the supernatant and fixed the volume to 5 mL for the enzyme extraction solution, which was used for the determination of the activities of sucrose synthase (SS), neutral invertase (NI), acid invertase (AI), and sucrose phosphate synthase (SPS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA Extraction, cDNA synthesis and quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction(qRT-PCR) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Total RNA was extracted from flowering Chinese cabbage leaves using RNeasy Mini kit (R4151-02B; Magen, Guangzhou, China) following the user’s instructions. Reverse transcriptase M−MLV (R212-01, Vazyme, Nanjing, China) was employed to synthesize first-strand cDNA. qRT-PCR using SYBR qPCR master mix kit (Q711-02, Vazyme, Nanjing, China) was performed with gene-specific primers on the Bio-Rad CFX96 Real-Time PCR System. qPCR was executed using 0.4 μL of forward and reverse primers (10 μM),10 μL 2 × ChamQ Universal SYBR qPCR Master Mix and 2 μL template cDNA in a total volume of 20 μL. The reaction conditions of qPCR were described as follows: 30 s at 95 ℃, 40 cycles of 5 s at 95 ℃ and 30 s at 60 ℃. The relative expression was assayed from the average values using the 2-ΔΔCt method. Three different biological samples and three technical replicates were used for all quantitative experiments. The BrActin was used as an internal control (Liu et al., 2013). All primers used in this work are shown in Table S1.

SPSS Statistics 25.0 software (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA) was performed for statistical analysis. Data were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD) with three replications. A Tukey’s test (P < 0.05) was used to assess the treatment effects. The figures were generated using Origin 2018c (OriginLab Corp., Northampton, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of SmCDs on the postharvest senescence of flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: Flowering Chinese cabbage is a typical leafy vegetable, and the yellowing of leaves is an obvious phenotype of plant senescence and decline in quality. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the phenomenon of leaf yellowing in the control group appeared from the 14 days of storage, and more serious yellowness of the leaves was observed on the 21 days. However, there were not visible yellowing leaves that could be detected under SmCDs treatment during 21 days of storage time. With an increase in storage time, the chlorophyll content was found to progressively decrease in both the control and SmCDs treatments, while SmCDs treatment could remarkably retard the reduction of chlorophyll content. After 7, 14, and 21 days of storage, the chlorophyll content of the control was decreased by 18.13 %, 17.58 %, and 49.45 % respectively. In contrast, the value of SmCDs treatment was reduced by 0.55 %, 5.49 %, and 38.46 %, respectively (Fig. 1B).

Chlorophyll fluorescence of the control was found to remain blue on the 7 days of storage and then found to turn green on the 14 days, while that of the SmCDs treatment was turn to green on the 21 days of storage, indicating that SmCDs could effectively inhibit the destruction of chlorophyll (Fig. 1C). With an increase in storage time, the Fv/Fm ratios of both control and SmCDs treatment were significantly decreased, but a lower degree of reduction was observed under SmCDs treatment. After 7, 14, and 21 days of storage, the Fv/Fm ratios of control were found to reduce by 4.73 %, 18.52 %, and 45.72 %, respectively, while the values of SmCDs treatment were decreased by 3.07 %, 8.43 %, and 16.35 %, respectively (Fig. 1D).

The relative expressions of six chlorophyll degradation-related genes (BrPAO, BrPPH, BrNYC1, BrNOL, BrNYE1, and BrRCCR) and one senescence marker gene (BrSAG12) were compared between the control and SmCDs treatments at different storage times. The results showed that the relative expressions of BrPAO, BrPPH, BrNYC1, BrNOL, BrNYE1, and BrSAG12 in control and SmCDs treatments were increased with the extension of storage time (Fig. 2 A-F). Moreover, the relative expressions of BrPAO, BrPPH, BrNYC1, BrNOL, BrNYE1, and BrSAG12 in control during 21 days of storage were higher than that in the SmCDs treatment. However, there were no statistically significant differences in the relative expression of BrRCCR between the control and SmCDs treatments on the 7 and 14 days of storage (Fig. 2G). These findings demonstrated that SmCDs could effectively delay senescence by inhibiting the chlorophyll degradation and suppressing the transcript levels of chlorophyll degradation-related genes (BrPAO, BrPPH, BrNYC1, BrNOL, and BrNYE1) and senescence marker gene (BrSAG12), while the BrRCCR might not be closely related to aging under SmCDs treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of SmCDs treatment on ROS metabolism in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: The identification of H2O2 was achieved by DAB staining, in which H2O2, catalyzed by peroxidase, reacted rapidly with DAB to form a brown compound, thus reflecting the amount of H2O2 accumulated in the leaf tissue. The superoxide anion O2 – was identified by NBT, which was reduced to water-insoluble blue dimethylation in the presence of O2 –, thereby showing the O2 – accumulation in the leaves. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the DAB and NBT staining results of the control and SmCDs-treated leaves displayed a relatively transparent white color at 0 d of storage. With the aging process, the brown color of DAB staining gradually deepened, and the blue color of NBT staining also gradually deepened. The control leaves had darker brown DAB and darker blue NBT staining, while the SmCDs-treated leaves had lighter brown DAB and weaker blue NBT staining. The findings implied that SmCDs had strong ability to scavenge ROS in the process of resisting the senescence of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage.

Furthermore, SmCDs treatment was found to significantly affect antioxidant metabolism. The cell membrane permeability of the control and SmCDs treatments increased with prolonged storage time, nevertheless, the degree of increase in SmCDs treatment was found to be lower than that in control. After storing for 7, 14, and 21 days, the cell membrane permeability of the control was increased by 39.81 %, 60.68 % and 137.98 %, respectively, while the value of SmCDs treatment was increased by 25.56 %, 42.60 %, and 56.42 %, respectively (Fig. 3B). The SOD activity was increased after storage in the control and SmCDs treatments, reached the highest value on the 7 days of storage and it was found to reduce after that. The SOD activity of SmCDs treatment was found to increase by 40.58 %, 41.78 %, and 58.29 % after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days compared to the control, respectively (Fig. 3C). The POD activity in control and SmCDs treatments decreased with an increase in storage time, and reached the lowest value on the 21 days, nevertheless, the value in SmCDs treatment was significantly higher than that in control. The POD activity of SmCDs treatment was increased by 12.05 %, 86.41 %, and 66.12 % after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days compared with the control, respectively (Fig. 3D). The longer storage time caused the decrease in CAT activity in both control and SmCDs treatments. The CAT activity reached a minimum on 14 days and it was found to be increased after that till 21 days of storage. The CAT activity was found to increase with SmCDs treatment by 27.33 %, 114.87 %, and 81.62 % after storing 7, 14, and 21 days (respectively) compared to the control (Fig. 3E). The APX activity was increased with a longer storage time in the control and SmCDstreatments. The maximum activity of APX was observed on the 21 days of storage. With SmCDs treatment, the APX activity was increased by 16.78 %, 38.08 %, and 62.76 % after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days (respectively) compared to the control (Fig. 3F). The LOX activity in control was enhanced with an increase in storage time and the maximum was detected on the 21 days, while the value was decreased in the SmCDs treatment, and its minimum was found on the 14 days before increasing on the 21 days (Fig. 3G). Furthermore, both the MDA content and Pro content were also increased with storage time and reached the highest value on the 21 days of storage. Compared with the control, SmCDs treatment caused the reduction in MDA content (8.19 %, 14.63 %, and 22.88 %) and Pro contents (10.98 %, 37.82 %, and 14.65 %) after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days (Fig. 3H, 3I).

In addition, the expressions of four respiratory burst oxidase homologs genes (BrRBohA, BrRBohB, BrRBohD, and BrRBohF) were shown in Fig. 4 . The expressions of BrRBohA, BrRBohB, and BrRBohD increased with leaf senescence in the SmCDs treatment and control group, reaching about 0.22, 9.00, and 11.67 folds of the initial level in the SmCDs treatment on the 21 days of storage respectively, and reaching about 111.11, 50.00, and 34.30 folds of the initial level in the control, respectively (Fig. 4A-C), which implied that SmCDs significantly depressed these genes transcript. Moreover, the expression of BrRBohF not only decreased during the storage, but was also significantly suppressed by SmCDs treatment (Fig. 4D). The above results exhibited that SmCDs could maintain the homeostasis of ROS metabolism through improving antioxidant enzyme activity, repressing the transcripts of respiratory burst oxidase homologs genes, lowering membrane lipid peroxidation, and protecting cell membrane structure, resulting in alleviating the damage of ROS to plants and delaying the senescence of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of SmCDs on the postharvest quality of flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: SmCDs treatment generally antagonized the weight loss of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. After 7, 14, and 21 days of storage, the weight loss in SmCDs treatment was 1.10 %, 1.40 %, and 2.40 %, respectively, while the value in control was 2.30 %, 1.70 %, and 2.50 %, respectively (Fig. 5 A). The vitamin C content in both control and SmCDs treatments showed a decreasing tendency during storage. With the SmCDs treatment, the vitamin C content was found to decrease by 2.50 %, 4.29 %, and 17.94 % after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days, respectively, and this value was found to be higher than that of the control (Fig. 5B). After 7, 14, and 21 days of storage, the soluble sugar content of SmCDs treatment was 13.74 %, 3.38 %, and 2.22 % higher than that of the control, respectively (Fig. 5C), Interestingly, with an increase in storage time, sucrose content was observed to decrease in the control, while it was detected to increase in the SmCDs treatment by 68.59 %, 55.90 %, and 71.25 % after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days compared to the control, respectively, indicating that SmCDs could significantly enhance the sucrose content during storage (Fig. 5D). During 21 days of storage, the change in flavonoid content was found to be consistent with that of sucrose content. In addition, the flavonoid content in the SmCDs treatment was increased by 19.67 %, 34.35 %, and 36.11 % after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days compared with the control, respectively, suggesting that SmCDs also could significantly increase the flavonoid content during storage (Fig. 5E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of SmCDs treatment on sugar metabolism in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: Given that SmCDs could promote the content of sucrose and soluble sugar, we further analyzed the activity of sugar metabolism-related enzymes in the leaves during 21 days of storage. As shown in the Fig. 6 , compared to the control, the NI activity of SmCDs treatment was increased by 44.72 %,102.03 %, and 415.91 % after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days, respectively (Fig. 6A). The results also demonstrated that SmCDs treatment correspondingly increased AI activity. Compared to the control, the AI activity of SmCDs treatment was observed to increase by 155.91 %, 200.60 %, and 120.95 % after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days, respectively (Fig. 6B). Moreover, SS activity gradually increased with an increase of storage time, and the SS activity of SmCDs treatment was significantly higher than that of the control, and increased by 8.77 %, 13.97 %, and 57.93 % after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days, respectively (Fig. 6C). However, SmCDs treatment likewise presented the ability to inhibit the activity of SPS, reducing it by 6.15 %, 9.39 % and 19.98 % compared to the control after storing for 7, 14, and 21 days of storage, respectively (Fig. 6D). Our findings demonstrated that SmCDs could enhance the NI, AI, and SS activities while suppressing the SPS activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of SmCDs treatment on flavonoid biosynthesis-related genes of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage
ce:section.ce:para: As pointing to the assumption that SmCDs treatment could considerably raise the flavonoid content, we further investigated that the impacts of SmCDs on seven flavonoid biosynthesis-related genes (BrFLS3, BrFLS1, BrC4H, BrCHI, BrDFR, BrCHS, and BrTT7). Our experimental data showed that SmCDs treatment had diverse effects on different flavonoid biosynthesis genes. The transcript level of BrFLS3 was significantly up-regulated by SmCDs treatment, and was 2.00 and 1.71 folds higher than that in the control on the 7 and 14 days of storage, respectively (Fig. 7 A). The expression level of BrFLS1 was significantly up-regulated 1.31 folds compared with the control on the 14 days of storage, but the difference was not observed on the 7 days of storage (Fig. 7B). SmCDs treatment also promoted the expression level of BrC4H, especially on the 14 days of storage, and was 2.28 folds higher than that of the control (Fig. 7C). The transcript level of BrCHI was significantly down-regulated in SmCDs treatment on the 7 days of storage, while it was significantly enhanced on the 14 days of storage and 1.59 folds higher than that of the control (Fig. 7D). The transcript level of BrDFR was significantly up-regulated in SmCDs treatment on the 7 days of storge compared with the control and it was 8.38 times higher than that of the control, but the difference was not noticed on the 14 days of storage (Fig. 7E). Additionally, the expression levels of BrCHS and BrTT7 were repressed by SmCDs treatment during storage. Compared to the control, the transcript level of BrCHS in SmCDs treatment were decreased by 73.17 % and 95.11 % on the 7 and 14 days of storage respectively, and the expression level of BrTT7 was reduced by 33.3 % on the 7 days of storage (Fig. 7F, 7G). However, there was no significant difference in the expression level of BrTT7 between control and SmCDs treatments on the 14 days of storage. These results suggested that SmCDs promoting flavonoid biosynthesis may be closely related to BrFLS3, BrFLS1, BrC4H, and BrCHI during the storage of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage.

The typical characteristic of leaf aging is yellowing, leading to nutritional degradation (Huang et al., 2022). Nanomaterials own beneficial functions in water vapor barrier properties, blocking CO2, ethylene elimination, and anti-microbial activity, which are proper for the preservation of postharvest agricultural products (Kanmani and Rhim, 2014). CDs, as a new type of nanomaterial, have a strong effect of antioxidation and resistance to stress (Fu et al., 2022), but whether it is involved in retarding the senescence of postharvest crops is still unidentified. The results of this work displayed that the leaf color in the control turned yellow from green after 14 days of storage. In contrast, the SmCDs-treated flowering Chinese cabbage remained almost green until the end of storage (21 days), indicating that SmCDs had better anti-aging effects. Many studies have described that the postharvest senescence could significantly be related to the chlorophyll degradation, the up-regulated expressions of chlorophyll degradation-related genes, and senescence genes (Aiamla-or et al., 2019). Postharvest chemical handling such as melatonin (Tan et al., 2019), 1-MCP (Gómez-Lobato et al., 2012), gibberellic acid (Xiao et al., 2022), and N-phenyl-N-(2-chloro-4-pyridyl) urea (Li et al., 2021) could be used to delay senescence by down-regulating the expressions of key chlorophyll catabolic-related genes and senescence-associated genes. These reports were similar to our findings showing that SmCDs significantly retarded the decomposition of chlorophyll and suppressed the transcript levels of key chlorophyll degradation-related genes (BrNOL, BrNYC1, BrNYE1, BrPAO, and BrPPH) and senescence marker gene (BrSAG12). Although SmCDs could significantly postpone the aging of flowering Chinese cabbage after harvest, the expression of the chlorophyll catabolic gene BrRCCR was not found to retard during the senescence, suggesting that the BrRCCR might not be closely associated with senescence under SmCDs treatment. Our present result was similar to the findings reported by Gómez-Lobato et al. (2012) and Wei et al. (2019). We further observed that SmCDs significantly reduced the weight loss, which might be due to the facts that SmCDs could enhance the water potential of leaves (Kou et al., 2021), and reduce the water loss (Li et al., 2021). These findings indicated that SmCDs had a positive role in extending the postharvest shelf life and delaying the senescence of harvested flowering Chinese cabbage by hindering the transcript levels of key genes associated with chlorophyll degradation and senescence, and reducing water loss.

Membrane deterioration or damage is a typical physiological characteristic of plant senescence. When plants are under stress, the ROS metabolism is disordered and the free radical equilibrium is destroyed, resulting in an increase in the accumulation of ROS, which triggers and aggravates membrane lipid peroxidation, and can cause the damage of cell membrane and even plant death (Shabala et al., 2014). Plants can equilibrate ROS metabolism by activating the activities of the antioxidant systems. Antioxidant enzymes such as SOD, APX, CAT, and POD played vital roles in impeding free radical formation and scavenging free radicals in the antioxidant systems, contributed to reducing membrane lipid peroxidation, maintaining membrane structure and function, and protecting the plant from damage (Mao et al., 2022). Previous studies have demonstrated that CDs can activate the antioxidant system activities of several crops to resist stress (Kou et al., 2021; Gohari et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021). However, the roles of CDs in delaying senescence by maintaining ROS homeostasis remain largely unidentified in harvested vegetables. The initiation of plant aging has been shown to be closely related to ROS homeostasis (Fu et al., 2019). Previous reports have manifested that promoting the antioxidant system efficiency to reduce ROS excessive formation was beneficial in delaying leaf senescence of crops (Ahmad et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2012). Nitric oxide treatment promoted the activity of SOD, CAT, and APX, but reduced cell membrane permeability and MDA content, inhibiting the production of superoxide anion and reducing the peroxide (H2O2) content (Zhao et al., 2020). In our current study, the SmCDs-treated leaves had lighter brown DAB and weaker blue NBT staining compared to the control, indicating that SmCDs had strong ability to scavenge ROS in resisting the senescence of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. Alginate, chitosan, and carrageenan coating treatments delayed the leaf senescence of lettuce by enhancing the activities of antioxidant enzymes (SOD, POD, CAT, and APX), while reducing LOX activity and lowering the content of MDA (Li et al., 2021). Other reports also showed that γ-irradiation treatment could promote the activities of CAT, POD, and SOD, but decrease the LOX activity, MDA content and permeability of cell membranes, thus prolonging the shelf life of blueberry (Wang et al., 2017). Moreover, 1-MCP or salicylic acid delayed floret senescence by enhancing the activities of antioxidant enzymes such as CAT, SOD, and POD, and reducing both proline content and MDA level (Hassan and Ali, 2014). These findings were similar to our results exhibiting that SmCDs could promote the activities of antioxidant enzymes (CAT, SOD, POD, and APX), inhibit the activity of LOX, and reduce the contents of proline and MDA as well as the permeability of cell membranes, which further confirmed that SmCDs could effectively scavenge ROS and protect the plants from membrane lipid peroxidation and damage, thus delaying the aging of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. Furthermore, respiratory burst oxidase homologues (RBOHs) are the most widely explored enzymatic source of ROS, and play pivotal roles in regulating ROS production (Wang et al., 2019). Some studies have testified that most ROS signaling and generation during aging depends on the RBOHs activities and their transcript levels of the encoding genes (Marino et al., 2012). Tan et al. (2020) reported that melatonin delaying postharvest leaf senescence may be related to maintaining ROS homeostasis through promoting the antioxidant enzymes (POD, and SOD) activities and repressing RBOHs-catalyzed ROS production. Our present study had similar findings showing that SmCDs remarkably down-regulated the expressions of BrRBohA, BrRBohB, BrRBohD, and BrRBohF in the postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage leaves, indicating that SmCDs could effectively inhibit the excessive production of ROS to maintain ROS homeostasis. Based on the above discussed findings, it might be presumed that SmCDs possessed strong antioxidant capacity, and delayed the postharvest senescence by promoting the activity of antioxidant enzymes and scavenging capacity of ROS, suppressing the transcripts of RBOHs to maintain ROS metabolism homeostasis, reducing membrane lipid peroxidation, and protecting the cell membrane from damage in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage.

CDs can effectively increase vitamin C contents of lettuces and tomatoes, however, they show various impacts on different crops (Kou et al., 2021). CDs have also been found to maintain the vitamin C content of fresh-cut cucumbers (Fan et al., 2020). These finding are consistent with our results showing that SmCDs similarly increased the content of vitamin C in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. Sugar is an important quality factor of horticultural crops, the content of most horticultural products is degraded easily after harvest resulting in a decrease in postharvest quality (Hasperué et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2016). Therefore, to better preserve sugar content of harvested horticultural products has become an important issue to be solved in horticultural industry. Previous reports exhibited that CDs could enhance the sugar content in plants (Hu et al., 2022; Kou et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021). We similarly found that SmCDs also increased the contents of sucrose and soluble sugar during postharvest storage. Even so, the roles of SmCDs involving in sugar metabolism currently remain vague in postharvest horticultural crops. It is well known that the sugar content in plants is closely related to the sugar metabolism-related enzyme activities. NI, AI, SPS, and SS are key enzymes that regulate the metabolism of sugar in plants, however, the roles of these enzymes were different in different crops (Tan et al., 2022). The accumulations of fructose and sucrose were associated with an increase in the activities of SS, NI, and AI, while the glucose content exhibited opposite impacts under sucrose treatment in sugarcane (Lobo et al., 2015). The other works showed that NI and AI played a key role in the soluble sugar accumulation under selenium treatment alone, and AI might be the pivotal enzyme to restrict the accumulation of sugar under combined treatments of selenium and sucrose in pea sprouts (Tan et al., 2022). Wang et al. (2020) showed that lower sucrose content but higher values of glucose and fructose contents were determined as the extending of storage time, evidenced by the reduction in activities of SPS and SS with an increase in invertase activities. Postharvest chemical treatment can effectively regulate the sugar metabolism of horticultural products. Previous studies have displayed that the applications of 6-benzylaminopurine, sodium nitroprusside and adenosine triphosphate after harvest improved the activities of SS and SPS, while they repressed the activities of AI, SS-c, and NI, thus maintaining the postharvest quality of apples (Chen et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2020). Additionally, 1-MCP could retard aging of broccoli by regulating the metabolism of sugar to maintain higher content of sugar (Xu et al., 2016). Nevertheless, Deng et al. (2013) indicated that postharvest NO treatment could repress SS and SPS activities to delay the fruit senescence of apple. Ren et al. (2021) further described that the activity of AI was not significantly related to the content of sucrose during fruit development. These findings exhibited that the different treatments had diverse effects on the metabolism of sugar in different plants. In this study, SmCDs could increase the contents of sucrose and soluble sugar during storage, as well as improve the activities of SS, AI, and NI but it was found to suppress the SPS activity in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. Thus, we speculated SmCDs maintained higher sugar content during postharvest senescence might be closely associated with promoting the activities of NI, AI, and SS.

Flavonoid is an important active phytochemical and antioxidant that is widely found in plants benefiting to human health, however, it is found to straightforwardly be degradable after harvest (Cavaiuolo et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020; Yue et al., 2022). Previous studies showed that some postharvest treatments such as melatonin, chlorine dioxide, 1-MCP, and benzoic acid could postpone aging to retard the flavonoid degradation in horticultural crops (Liang et al., 2018; Yue et al., 2022). In this work, we also found that SmCDs could remarkably promote the flavonoid accumulation compared to the control during storage in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. Different treatments such as chemical, physical methods have been reported to have diverse effects on postharvest flavonoid biosynthesis (Reyes Jara et al., 2022), and changes of flavonoid content were closely associated with genes related to flavonoid synthesis. Wang et al. (2020) indicated that the expression levels of genes related to flavonoid biosynthesis (CHI, 4CL, C4H, and PAL) were repressed during cold storage of fruits, which was accompanied by decreased contents of flavonoid. Previous findings suggested that melatonin delayed the aging of kiwifruit leaves by regulating expressions of AaPAL, AaC4H, AaCHS, AaFNS, AaF3H, AaLAR, and AaANR, thus improving flavonoid contents (Liang et al., 2018). Furthermore, UV-C treatment has been reported to induce the transcripts of genes encoding key enzymes in the phenylpropane pathway, including PAL, C4H, CHI, 4CL, F3H, CHS, and FLS, thereby increasing the biosynthesis of flavonoid in tomatoes (Liu et al., 2018). As far as this, there is still no clarification of CDs in regulating flavonoid biosynthesis-related genes. In this study, we observed that SmCDs treatment evidently improved the accumulation of flavonoid. Besides, we assayed the transcript levels of flavonoid biosynthesis-related genes (BrFLS3, BrFLS1, BrC4H, BrCHI, BrDFR, BrCHS, and BrTT7), and found that SmCDs were also involved in the regulation of these genes and the transcript levels of BrFLS3, BrFLS1, BrC4H, and BrCHI were significantly up-regulated by SmCDs treatment during storage. These results suggested that SmCDs could promote the flavonoid content and the promotion of flavonoid biosynthesis may be related to the up-regulation of BrFLS3, BrFLS1, BrC4H, and BrCHI under SmCDs treatment in postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage.

In summary, SmCDs have represented promising antioxidant and anti-aging effects during storage of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. SmCDs could effectively inhibit the chlorophyll degradation, repress the transcripts of BrNYE1, BrNYC1, BrNOL, BrPPH, BrPAO, and BrSAG12, sustain the homeostasis of ROS metabolism by increasing the activities of SOD, CAT, POD, and APX and hindering the transcripts of BrRBohA, BrRBohB, BrRBohD, and BrRBohF, lower membrane lipid peroxidation and protect cell membrane structure from damage, resulting in delaying the senescence and improving the contents of vitamin C, sucrose, soluble sugar, and flavonoid of postharvest flowering Chinese cabbage. Moreover, SmCDs could promote the sugar accumulation by positively regulating the activities of NI, AI, and SS, while enhance the flavonoid content by upregulating the expressions of BrFLS3, BrFLS1, BrC4H, and BrCHI. Our findings provided a new insight into the mechanism of SmCDs involved in maintaining postharvest quality by delaying the aging of flowering Chinese cabbage. These results might have important practical implications for improving commercial product qualities of flowering Chinese cabbage and greatly assist in the development of new postharvest handling method to extend the shelf life of horticultural crops.
